state\codex_consultations\plan_v6_brief.md:188:**Step 16.6 — CI nondeterminism tolerance** (lingering P1-12 / P1-14 robustness):
.codex\I-anti-003\codex_brief_verdict_iter_3.txt:8598:tests\unit\test_gemini_fixes.py:3795:        # FIX-164: Use robust extraction — find '[{' start, try ']' ends backward
.codex\I-anti-003\codex_brief_verdict_iter_3.txt:13098:.codex\I-bug-084\codex_brief_verdict_iter_1.txt:1431:.codex\I-f11-004\codex_diff_audit_iter_1.txt:3879:.codex/I-f11-004\codex_brief_verdict_iter_2.txt:2908:.codex\I-f11-002\codex_diff_audit.txt:4013:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_TEST_091.json:7297:      "content": "Abstract\nSeveral studies have associated skipping (not having) breakfast with cardiometabolic risk factors such as obesity, high blood pressure, unfavorable lipid profiles, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome. We examined the available evidence regarding the effect of skipping breakfast on cardiovascular morbidity and mortality, as well as all-cause mortality. Medline, Embase, and Web of Science were searched from inception until May 2019 to identify prospective cohort studies that examined the association between skipping breakfast and the risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality and all-cause death. Electronic searches were supplemented by manual screening of the references of retrieved studies. Out of 456 citations identified, four studies (from Japan and the US) were included. The included studies involved a total of 199,634 adults (aged \u226540 years; 48.5% female) without known cardiovascular disease (CVD) at baseline followed over a median duration of 17.4 years. The pooled data suggested that people who regularly skipped breakfast were about 21% more likely (hazard ratio (HR) 1.21, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.08\u20131.35; I2 = 17.3%, p = 0.304) to experience incident CVD or die from it than people who regularly consumed breakfast. Also, the risk of all-cause death was 32% higher (HR 1.32, 95% CI 1.17\u20131.48; I2 = 7.6%, p = 0.339) in people who regularly skipped breakfast than in people who regularly consumed breakfast. However, the definition of skipping breakfast was heterogenous and adjustment for confounders varied significantly. Therefore, residual confounding could not be ruled out and caution is required in the interpretation of the findings. Hence, large prospective studies with a consistent definition of skipping breakfast, and conducted across different populations, are needed to provide more robust evidence of the health effects of skipping breakfast.\nKeywords: breakfast, meal frequency, cardiovascular disease, mortality\n1. Background\nCardiovascular diseases (CVDs) remain the leading contributors to disease burden and deaths worldwide. In 2015, there were more than 422 million cases of CVD, resulting in over 17.9 million deaths (~31% of all global deaths) [1]. Much of CVD is preventable through being physically active, not smoking, and consuming a healthy diet [2,3].\nWhilst evidence-based recommendations relative to eating habits (timing, quantity, energy content, and frequency) in adults are lacking in most nutritional guidelines [4,5], for many decades breakfast has been touted as the most important meal of the day [6]. Indeed, some have suggested that approximately 15\u201330% of daily energy intake should be consumed at breakfast [7,8]. Yet, more than a quarter of adults skip this presumably most important meal of the day [9,10,11]. Skipping breakfast is notably common in socioeconomically disadvantaged people, shift workers or those with excessive working hours, as well as people with disinterest in food (such as those with depression), and those with poor health literacy [12,13,14].\nEpidemiological studies have demonstrated a higher prevalence and incidence of cardiometabolic risk factors such as obesity, high blood pressure, unfavorable lipid profiles, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome in people who skip breakfast, which may ultimately contribute to increased risk of CVD [15,16,17]. Recently, Uzhova and colleagues reported that, compared with consuming a high-energy breakfast, regularly skipping breakfast was associated with a 1.6- and 2.6-fold higher likelihood of non-coronary and generalized atherosclerosis, respectively, after controlling for conventional cardiovascular risk factors and diet quality [18].\nHowever, the exact magnitude of the effect of skipping breakfast on the risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality, and all-cause death, remains poorly understood. Improved understanding of the impact of skipping breakfast on the onset of CVD and related death may help to inform guideline development and public health interventions. Thus, we conducted a systematic review to examine the available evidence regarding the effect of skipping breakfast on the risk of incident CVD and mortality, as well as all-cause death, in the primary prevention setting.\n2. Methods\nThis systematic review followed the methods outlined by the Cochrane collaboration, as well as the Guidelines of the Preferred Reporting for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement [19].\n2.1. Search Strategy and Study Selection\nWe searched Medline, Embase, and Web of Science from inception until 5 May 2019 for studies published in English that reported the prospective association between skipping breakfast and the risk of CVD development and mortality as well as all-cause death. The following search terms were used in combination: (\u201ccardiovascular disease\u201d OR \u201ccardiovascular disorders\u201d OR \u201ccoronary heart disease\u201d OR \u201ccoronary artery disease\u201d OR \u201cacute coronary syndrome\u201d OR \u201cischemic heart disease\u201d OR \u201cmyocardial infarction\u201d OR \u201cmyocardial ischemia\u201d OR \u201cheart attack\u201d OR \u201cheart failure\u201d OR \u201cstroke\u201d or \u201ccerebrovascular disease\u201d OR \u201ccardiac arrest\u201d OR \u201cunstable angina\u201d OR \u201cmortality\u201d OR \u201cdeath\u201d) AND (\u201cbreakfast\u201d OR \u201cbreakfast skipping\u201d OR \u201cskipping breakfast\u201d OR \u201cmorning meal\u201d OR \u201cmeal skipping\u201d OR \u201cmeal timing\u201d OR \u201cmeal frequency\u201d OR \u201ceating patterns\u201d OR \u201cmeal patterns\u201d OR \u201cfeeding behaviour\u201d OR \u201cmeal regularity\u201d) AND (\u201cobservational study\u201d OR \u201cprospective\u201d OR \u201ccohort\u201d OR \u201ccohorts\u201d OR \u201clongitudinal\u201d OR \u201cfollowup\u201d).\nStudies included had to investigate skipping breakfast as the exposure and the outcome had to be incident CVD, cardiovascular mortality, or all-cause death. The population of interest comprised adults without CVD at baseline. The results of the electronic database searches were first exported to Endnote and duplicates were removed. The title and abstract of each study were then screened. If the title or abstract seemed relevant, the full text of the record was evaluated. Only prospective cohort studies published in English were included. Conference abstracts, reviews and meta-analyses, editorials, non-English studies, studies with irrelevant exposures (e.g., combining skipping breakfast and late-night eating) or irrelevant outcomes, and study designs that could not estimate incidence (cross-sectional, case-control, and ecological studies) were excluded.\n2.2. Data Extraction, Quality Assessment, and Synthesis\nFor each study, the following data were extracted: name of first author, year of publication, the country where the study was conducted, the cohort name (if any), duration of follow-up, baseline characteristics of the cohort (mean age, percentage of females), and total number of participants. In addition, we extracted information on exposure (skipping breakfast) definition, exposure assessment (questionnaire with or without validation, interviews), and data on risk estimates expressed as relative risk (RRs) or hazard ratios (HRs) with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs), as well as the adjustment factors. Where available, we extracted risk estimates of composite CVD, as well as for specific conditions such as stroke and myocardial infarction. If studies defined skipping breakfast using frequencies rather than as a dichotomous exposure (yes vs. no), we selected the data comparing regular breakfast eating (every day) and never/rarely (0\u20132 days per week). Data were extracted by R.O. and cross-checked by A.O. All studies were read and assessed by two reviewers (R.O and A.O). There was 100% agreement between the two reviewers regarding the studies to be included or excluded. Each study\u2019s quality was assessed against the Newcastle\u2013Ottawa Scale [20]. Studies were ineligible for inclusion if they were rated below 5 on a scale from 0 to 9 following the checklist. The results of individual studies were pooled using random effects (DerSimonian and Laird) meta-analysis techniques. A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was performed to examine the stability of the pooled estimate. The statistical evidence of between-study heterogeneity was examined using the Cochran\u2019s Q test and the \u03992 statistic. I2 values of 25, 50, and 75% were considered to be low, moderate, and high degrees of heterogeneity, respectively. All analyses were performed using STATA version 15.0/IC (StataCorp, College Station, TX, USA). A p-value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant.\n3. Results\n3.1. Search Results and Study Characteristics\nThe electronic database searches retrieved 456 citations. After the removal of duplicates and titles and abstract screening, nine articles were selected for full text evaluation, of which four were included in the final review (Figure 1) [21,22,23,24]. Reasons for exclusion were conference abstract (n = 1), case-control study (n = 2), inappropriate exposure (n = 1), and inappropriate population (n = 1). The included studies were from Japan (n = 2) and the United States (n = 2). The studies involved a total of 199,634 adults aged \u226540 years (48.5% female) without known CVD at baseline. The sample size in individual studies ranged from 6550 to 83,410 participants. The mean duration of follow-up was 17.4 (range 12.7\u201319.4) years (Table 1). Data were collected from participants via self-administered questionnaires in three studies, whilst one study used home-based interviews. No study was excluded on the basis of quality assessment against the Newcastle\u2013Ottawa Scale.\nTable 1.\n| Author Details | Country of Study | Study (Cohort) Name | Sample Size | Age (Years) | % Female | Duration of Follow-Up (Years) | Breakfast Evaluation Method | Exposure (Breakfast) Definition | Outcome(s) |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Cahill et al., 2013 [21] | US | Health Professionals Follow-Up Study | 26,902 | 45\u201382 | 0.0 | 16.0 | Questionnaire (self-administered) | Breakfast was defined as a positive response to any of the first three eating times (\u2018before breakfast\u2019, \u2018breakfast\u2019, \u2018between breakfast and lunch\u2019) | Incident coronary heart disease (CHD); defined as non-fatal myocardial infarction (MI) or fatal CHD |\n| Kubota et al., 2016 [22] | Japan | The Japan Public Health Center-Based Prospective (JPHC) study | 82,772 | 45\u201374 | 53.3 | 12.7 | Questionnaire (self-administered) | Participants were classified into the following four groups; those who had breakfast 0 to 2 (subjects with almost never, those with 1\u20133 times/month, and those with 1\u20132 times/week were combined because of the small number of those with 1\u20133 times/month or 1\u20132 times/week), 3\u20134, 5\u20136, or 7 (everyday) times/week. Those who had breakfast 7 times/week were regarded as the reference group. | Stroke and CHD (i.e., myocardial infarction and sudden cardiac death) |\n| Rong et al., 2019 [23] | US | National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey III | 6550 | 40\u201375 | 52.0 | 18.8 | Home-based interviews | Participants were asked \u201cHow often do you eat breakfast?\u201d during the household interview, and the possible answers included \u201cevery day,\u201d \u201csome days,\u201d \u201crarely,\u201d \u201cnever,\u201d and \u201cweekends only.\u201d The frequency of breakfast eating was classified as \u201cnever,\u201d \u201crarely,\u201d \u201csome days,\u201d or \u201cevery day.\u201d | Death from cardiovascular disease (CVD) (defined as heart disease or stroke; ICD codes: (I00\u201309, I11, I13, I20\u201351, I60\u201369), heart disease, stroke, or all-cause death. |\n| Yokoyama et al., 2016 [24] | Japan | The Japan Collaborative Cohort Study (JACC) Study | 83,410 | 40\u201379 | 59.1 | 19.4 | Questionnaire (self-administered) | The type of breakfast consumed was assessed according to the following five categories: Japanese style, Western style, Chagayu style (tea rice gruel), no or nearly no breakfast eaten, or other. Participants were classified into two groups, as those who eat breakfast (including Japanese style, Western style, Chagayu style (tea rice gruel), and other) and those who skip breakfast (no or nearly no breakfast eaten). | Deaths from circulatory diseases (I00\u2013I99) or all-cause death. |\n3.2. Association of Skipping Breakfast with CVD Morbidity and Mortality\nCahill et al. [21] reported that in age-adjusted models, men who did not eat breakfast had a 33% increased risk of coronary heart disease (CHD) compared with men who did (risk ratio (RR) = 1.33, 95% CI 1.13\u20131.57). This risk was similar (RR 1.27, 95% CI 1.06\u20131.53) when also adjusted for diet, demographic, and activity factors, but was attenuated (RR 1.18, 95% CI 0.98\u20131.43) when further adjusted for body mass index (BMI), hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, and diabetes.\nKubota and colleagues [22] also reported an inverse association between the frequency of breakfast intake and the risks of total CVD, total stroke, and cerebral hemorrhage, after adjusting for dietary and lifestyle factors. Compared to people who consumed breakfast seven times per week, those who consumed breakfast zero to two times per week were 14% (hazard ratio (HR) 1.14, 1.01\u20131.27), 18% (HR 1.18, 1.04\u20131.34), and 36% (HR 1.36, 95% CI 1.10\u20131.70) more likely to develop CVD, stroke, and cerebral hemorrhage, respectively. However, the authors observed no significant association between the frequency of eating breakfast and the risk of CHD, although, they attributed this to the relatively low prevalence of CHD in the Japanese population, leading to the low power.\nUsing data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES), Rong et al. [23] reported that skipping breakfast was significantly associated with the risk of cardiovascular mortality, especially stroke-specific mortality, which persisted after adjusting for demographic, socioeconomic, dietary, and lifestyle factors, BMI, and cardiovascular risk factors. Specifically, compared to people who reported consuming breakfast every day, those reporting never consuming breakfast were 1.87 and 3.34 times more likely to die from CVD or stroke, respectively.\nBased on data from the Japan Collaborative Cohort (JACC) Study for Evaluation of Cancer Risks, Yokoyama and colleagues [24] reported that skipping breakfast was associated with an increased risk of death from circulatory diseases in men (HR 1.42, 95% CI 1.02\u20132.02), but not women (HR 1.19, 95% CI 0.71\u20131.05), after adjusting for medical, sociodemographic, and lifestyle factors.\nOverall, pooling data from the four included studies suggested that people who regularly skipped breakfast were 21% more likely to experience CVD or die from it than people who ate breakfast regularly (HR 1.21, 95% CI 1.08\u20131.35; I2 = 17.3%, p = 0.304) (Figure 2). A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis suggested a variation of the effect size from 1.17 to 1.25, which were all statistically significant towards an increased risk of CVD morbidity and mortality among people who regularly skipped breakfast compared to those who ate it regularly.\n3.3. Association of Skipping Breakfast with All-Cause Mortality\nTwo prospective cohort studies reported the association between skipping breakfast and the risk of all-cause death. Rong et al. [23] found a non-significant increase in the risk of all-cause death among people who never ate breakfast compared to those who consumed breakfast every day (HR 1.19, 95% CI 0.99\u20131.42). Nonetheless, a Japanese study found that skipping breakfast was associated with increased risk of all-cause death in both males (HR 1.43, 95% CI 1.21\u20131.69) and females (HR 1.34, 95% CI 1.04\u20131.73) [24]. The pooled estimates from these two studies (3 data points) showed a 32% higher risk of death from all causes among people who regularly skipped breakfast compared to those who regularly consumed it (HR 1.32, 95% CI 1.17\u20131.48; I2 = 7.6%, p = 0.339). A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis suggested a variation of the effect size from 1.24 to 1.40, which were all statistically significant towards an increased risk of all-cause death among people who regularly skipped breakfast compared to those who ate it regularly.\n4. Discussion\nNutrition plays an important role in the maintenance of overall health [25,26,27]. Recently, a statement from the American Heart Association (AHA) underscored the substantive role of meal timing and frequency in the prevention of CVDs [28].\nIn this study, we reviewed the evidence regarding the association between skipping breakfast and the prospective risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality and all-cause death. The magnitudes of association were variable across studies but overall suggested that people who skip breakfast may be at greater risk of experiencing adverse health outcomes compared to people who regularly consume breakfast. Specifically, we found that people who regularly skipped breakfast were about 21% more likely to suffer a CVD event or die from it, and 32% more likely to die from all causes than people who regularly ate breakfast.\nAlthough our analysis focused on studies performed within CVD primary preventive settings, two studies that reported the effect of skipping breakfast on all-cause mortality in non-primary-preventive settings also showed that skipping breakfast alone or in combination with late night dinner was associated with increased risk of all-cause death [29,30]. Among patients with ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction, skipping breakfast concomitant with late night dinner was associated with 4\u20135-fold increased likelihood of death, reinfarction, and postinfarction angina within 30 days after hospital discharge [30].\nOmitting breakfast has been associated with obesity, hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation, and may also impair serum lipids and postprandial insulin sensitivity [8,15,16,17,31,32]. Recently, skipping breakfast has also been shown to have an adverse effect on arterial stiffness and carotid atheromatic burden [33]. Furthermore, in mice, skipping breakfast has been shown to result in a disturbance of the phase peak of the clock gene, and an inability to entrain to the day/night cycle, thereby ultimately leading to disturbance of the circadian rhythm [24,34]. All these mechanisms may underlie the increased risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality among people who skip breakfast compared to those who regularly consume it. Nonetheless, studies suggest that eating breakfast may be a marker of lifestyle pattern [35,36,37]. For example, people who skip breakfast are more likely to smoke, have lower physical activity, low levels of total energy intake, eat dinner at irregular times and snack every day, or may have higher intakes of red and processed meat, appetizers, and alcohol during the rest of the day [37,38]. Skipping breakfast has also been related to sleep duration and shift work, both of which have been shown to increase the risk of CVD [14,39,40]. These factors may therefore potentially confound the association between skipping breakfast and the risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality as well as all-cause death.\nWhile most studies attempted to adjust for potential confounders, data availability meant that there was significant variation in the confounders being adjusted for. Thus, residual confounding remains an issue [41] and should be considered when interpreting and comparing the findings of studies. For example, Kubota and colleagues [22] adjusted for sleep duration and perceived mental stress in their regression models, but such factors were not adjusted for in the analysis by Rong et al. [23]. It is known that short sleep duration is associated with increased risk of CHD mortality [42]. Moreover, Kubota and colleagues [22] argued that because obesity, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and diabetes mellitus could be mediators of the association between breakfast and CVD risk, they did not adjust for these in their regression analysis. However, these factors were included in the models presented by Rong et al. [23], Cahill et al. [21], and Yokoyama et al. [24]. These disparities in analytic framework may have contributed to the observed differences in the results. An equally important point also is that in nearly all the studies included in this review, the authors collected information on the frequency of breakfast intake only at baseline. Therefore, it is possible that participants might have changed their eating habits during the follow-up, which potentially introduces misclassification bias.\nThe finding of Yokoyama et al. [24] that skipping breakfast was associated with increased risk of death from circulatory diseases in men but not in women suggests possible gender differences in the effect of skipping breakfast. At the Experimental Biology meeting in Boston in 2015, Cahill and colleagues presented preliminary findings from the 56,593 women in the Nurses\u2019 Health Study which showed that skipping breakfast was associated with increased risk of CHD in people aged <60 years but not in those aged 60 years and older [43]. Furthermore, a pioneering longitudinal study by Kaplan and colleagues found that skipping breakfast was associated with an increased risk of all-cause mortality in people aged \u226570 years but not in those aged less than 70 years [29]. Moreover, age and gender-related differences in the patterns of breakfast consumption have been observed [44,45]. Thus, future studies should explore in detail the potential gender and age-related differences in the association between skipping breakfast and the risk of adverse health outcomes.\nIt is important to also highlight that the included studies were from Japan and the US, two countries with very different cultural contexts and eating habits [46,47]. Landmark studies such as the NiHonSan study, which compared cardiovascular risk in Japanese men living in Japan, Hawaii, and California following post-World War II migration, illustrated the importance of environmental and dietary behavioral factors as contributors to cardiovascular risk, and found that living in a \u2018Western\u2019 environment was associated with increased CV risk [48]. The traditional Japanese breakfast of soup and side dishes containing soybean products, seafood, or vegetables [49] differs markedly from US breakfasts which are commonly high in refined grains and milk [11]. Regardless, due to the globalization of the food supply, some shifts away from traditional Japanese breakfasts have been observed among some populations in Japan [50,51].\nAnother issue relates to what constituted breakfast and how skipping breakfast was defined in the included studies. While some defined skipping breakfast on the basis of consumption, others based their definition on the basis of timing [21]. The definition of what constitutes breakfast is controversial [8,52]. In particular, there are concerns, for example, that using the time of day as a means of defining meals is restrictive to specific cultures and inappropriate for some population subgroups such as night or shift workers [14,28]. O\u2019Neil and colleagues proposed a more unified definition of breakfast as \u201cthe first meal of the day that breaks the fast after the longest period of sleep, occurs within 2 to 3 h of awakening, and contains foods and beverages from at least one food group\u201d [53], a definition that has been adopted by the AHA [28]. To improve the comparability of future studies, uniform definitions of both breakfast and skipping breakfast are required. Lastly, the included studies did not examine whether different types of foods or beverages eaten at breakfast have different effects on cardiovascular and overall health; this should be investigated in future studies.\n5. Conclusions\nPooled data from a small number of published prospective cohort studies from the US and Japan suggest that skipping breakfast is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease morbidity and mortality as well as all-cause death. However, the definition of skipping breakfast was highly heterogenous and residual confounding factors pose a challenge for the interpretation of available data. Large prospective studies that utilize consistent definitions of skipping breakfast and are conducted across different populations are needed to provide more robust evidence of the health effects of skipping breakfast.\nAuthor Contributions\nR.O.-A., A.J.O. and D.L. contributed to study conception. R.O.-A. and A.J.O. performed studies\u2019 screening. R.O.-A. conducted the analysis and wrote the first draft. All authors contributed to manuscript revision and approved the final version.\nFunding\nThis research received no external funding\nConflicts of Interest\nThe authors declare no relevant conflict of interest.\nReferences\n- 1.Roth G.A., Johnson C., Abajobir A., Abd-Allah F., Abera S.F., Abyu G., Ahmed M., Aksut B., Alam T., Alam K., et al. Global, Regional, and National Burden of Cardiovascular Diseases for 10 Causes, 1990 to 2015. J. Am. Coll Cardiol. 2017;70:1\u201325. doi: 10.1016/j.jacc.2017.04.052. [DOI] [PMC free article] [PubMed] [Google Scholar]\n- 2.GBD 2017 Diet Collaborators Health effects of dietary risks in 195 countries, 1990\u20132017: A systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2017. Lancet. 2019;393:"
.codex\I-anti-003\codex_brief_verdict_iter_3.txt:13127:.codex\I-bug-084\codex_brief_verdict_iter_1.txt:1460:.codex\I-f11-004\codex_diff_audit_iter_1.txt:11065:.codex\I-f11-002\codex_diff_audit.txt:4013:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_TEST_091.json:7297:      "content": "Abstract\nSeveral studies have associated skipping (not having) breakfast with cardiometabolic risk factors such as obesity, high blood pressure, unfavorable lipid profiles, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome. We examined the available evidence regarding the effect of skipping breakfast on cardiovascular morbidity and mortality, as well as all-cause mortality. Medline, Embase, and Web of Science were searched from inception until May 2019 to identify prospective cohort studies that examined the association between skipping breakfast and the risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality and all-cause death. Electronic searches were supplemented by manual screening of the references of retrieved studies. Out of 456 citations identified, four studies (from Japan and the US) were included. The included studies involved a total of 199,634 adults (aged \u226540 years; 48.5% female) without known cardiovascular disease (CVD) at baseline followed over a median duration of 17.4 years. The pooled data suggested that people who regularly skipped breakfast were about 21% more likely (hazard ratio (HR) 1.21, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.08\u20131.35; I2 = 17.3%, p = 0.304) to experience incident CVD or die from it than people who regularly consumed breakfast. Also, the risk of all-cause death was 32% higher (HR 1.32, 95% CI 1.17\u20131.48; I2 = 7.6%, p = 0.339) in people who regularly skipped breakfast than in people who regularly consumed breakfast. However, the definition of skipping breakfast was heterogenous and adjustment for confounders varied significantly. Therefore, residual confounding could not be ruled out and caution is required in the interpretation of the findings. Hence, large prospective studies with a consistent definition of skipping breakfast, and conducted across different populations, are needed to provide more robust evidence of the health effects of skipping breakfast.\nKeywords: breakfast, meal frequency, cardiovascular disease, mortality\n1. Background\nCardiovascular diseases (CVDs) remain the leading contributors to disease burden and deaths worldwide. In 2015, there were more than 422 million cases of CVD, resulting in over 17.9 million deaths (~31% of all global deaths) [1]. Much of CVD is preventable through being physically active, not smoking, and consuming a healthy diet [2,3].\nWhilst evidence-based recommendations relative to eating habits (timing, quantity, energy content, and frequency) in adults are lacking in most nutritional guidelines [4,5], for many decades breakfast has been touted as the most important meal of the day [6]. Indeed, some have suggested that approximately 15\u201330% of daily energy intake should be consumed at breakfast [7,8]. Yet, more than a quarter of adults skip this presumably most important meal of the day [9,10,11]. Skipping breakfast is notably common in socioeconomically disadvantaged people, shift workers or those with excessive working hours, as well as people with disinterest in food (such as those with depression), and those with poor health literacy [12,13,14].\nEpidemiological studies have demonstrated a higher prevalence and incidence of cardiometabolic risk factors such as obesity, high blood pressure, unfavorable lipid profiles, diabetes, and metabolic syndrome in people who skip breakfast, which may ultimately contribute to increased risk of CVD [15,16,17]. Recently, Uzhova and colleagues reported that, compared with consuming a high-energy breakfast, regularly skipping breakfast was associated with a 1.6- and 2.6-fold higher likelihood of non-coronary and generalized atherosclerosis, respectively, after controlling for conventional cardiovascular risk factors and diet quality [18].\nHowever, the exact magnitude of the effect of skipping breakfast on the risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality, and all-cause death, remains poorly understood. Improved understanding of the impact of skipping breakfast on the onset of CVD and related death may help to inform guideline development and public health interventions. Thus, we conducted a systematic review to examine the available evidence regarding the effect of skipping breakfast on the risk of incident CVD and mortality, as well as all-cause death, in the primary prevention setting.\n2. Methods\nThis systematic review followed the methods outlined by the Cochrane collaboration, as well as the Guidelines of the Preferred Reporting for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement [19].\n2.1. Search Strategy and Study Selection\nWe searched Medline, Embase, and Web of Science from inception until 5 May 2019 for studies published in English that reported the prospective association between skipping breakfast and the risk of CVD development and mortality as well as all-cause death. The following search terms were used in combination: (\u201ccardiovascular disease\u201d OR \u201ccardiovascular disorders\u201d OR \u201ccoronary heart disease\u201d OR \u201ccoronary artery disease\u201d OR \u201cacute coronary syndrome\u201d OR \u201cischemic heart disease\u201d OR \u201cmyocardial infarction\u201d OR \u201cmyocardial ischemia\u201d OR \u201cheart attack\u201d OR \u201cheart failure\u201d OR \u201cstroke\u201d or \u201ccerebrovascular disease\u201d OR \u201ccardiac arrest\u201d OR \u201cunstable angina\u201d OR \u201cmortality\u201d OR \u201cdeath\u201d) AND (\u201cbreakfast\u201d OR \u201cbreakfast skipping\u201d OR \u201cskipping breakfast\u201d OR \u201cmorning meal\u201d OR \u201cmeal skipping\u201d OR \u201cmeal timing\u201d OR \u201cmeal frequency\u201d OR \u201ceating patterns\u201d OR \u201cmeal patterns\u201d OR \u201cfeeding behaviour\u201d OR \u201cmeal regularity\u201d) AND (\u201cobservational study\u201d OR \u201cprospective\u201d OR \u201ccohort\u201d OR \u201ccohorts\u201d OR \u201clongitudinal\u201d OR \u201cfollowup\u201d).\nStudies included had to investigate skipping breakfast as the exposure and the outcome had to be incident CVD, cardiovascular mortality, or all-cause death. The population of interest comprised adults without CVD at baseline. The results of the electronic database searches were first exported to Endnote and duplicates were removed. The title and abstract of each study were then screened. If the title or abstract seemed relevant, the full text of the record was evaluated. Only prospective cohort studies published in English were included. Conference abstracts, reviews and meta-analyses, editorials, non-English studies, studies with irrelevant exposures (e.g., combining skipping breakfast and late-night eating) or irrelevant outcomes, and study designs that could not estimate incidence (cross-sectional, case-control, and ecological studies) were excluded.\n2.2. Data Extraction, Quality Assessment, and Synthesis\nFor each study, the following data were extracted: name of first author, year of publication, the country where the study was conducted, the cohort name (if any), duration of follow-up, baseline characteristics of the cohort (mean age, percentage of females), and total number of participants. In addition, we extracted information on exposure (skipping breakfast) definition, exposure assessment (questionnaire with or without validation, interviews), and data on risk estimates expressed as relative risk (RRs) or hazard ratios (HRs) with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs), as well as the adjustment factors. Where available, we extracted risk estimates of composite CVD, as well as for specific conditions such as stroke and myocardial infarction. If studies defined skipping breakfast using frequencies rather than as a dichotomous exposure (yes vs. no), we selected the data comparing regular breakfast eating (every day) and never/rarely (0\u20132 days per week). Data were extracted by R.O. and cross-checked by A.O. All studies were read and assessed by two reviewers (R.O and A.O). There was 100% agreement between the two reviewers regarding the studies to be included or excluded. Each study\u2019s quality was assessed against the Newcastle\u2013Ottawa Scale [20]. Studies were ineligible for inclusion if they were rated below 5 on a scale from 0 to 9 following the checklist. The results of individual studies were pooled using random effects (DerSimonian and Laird) meta-analysis techniques. A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was performed to examine the stability of the pooled estimate. The statistical evidence of between-study heterogeneity was examined using the Cochran\u2019s Q test and the \u03992 statistic. I2 values of 25, 50, and 75% were considered to be low, moderate, and high degrees of heterogeneity, respectively. All analyses were performed using STATA version 15.0/IC (StataCorp, College Station, TX, USA). A p-value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant.\n3. Results\n3.1. Search Results and Study Characteristics\nThe electronic database searches retrieved 456 citations. After the removal of duplicates and titles and abstract screening, nine articles were selected for full text evaluation, of which four were included in the final review (Figure 1) [21,22,23,24]. Reasons for exclusion were conference abstract (n = 1), case-control study (n = 2), inappropriate exposure (n = 1), and inappropriate population (n = 1). The included studies were from Japan (n = 2) and the United States (n = 2). The studies involved a total of 199,634 adults aged \u226540 years (48.5% female) without known CVD at baseline. The sample size in individual studies ranged from 6550 to 83,410 participants. The mean duration of follow-up was 17.4 (range 12.7\u201319.4) years (Table 1). Data were collected from participants via self-administered questionnaires in three studies, whilst one study used home-based interviews. No study was excluded on the basis of quality assessment against the Newcastle\u2013Ottawa Scale.\nTable 1.\n| Author Details | Country of Study | Study (Cohort) Name | Sample Size | Age (Years) | % Female | Duration of Follow-Up (Years) | Breakfast Evaluation Method | Exposure (Breakfast) Definition | Outcome(s) |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Cahill et al., 2013 [21] | US | Health Professionals Follow-Up Study | 26,902 | 45\u201382 | 0.0 | 16.0 | Questionnaire (self-administered) | Breakfast was defined as a positive response to any of the first three eating times (\u2018before breakfast\u2019, \u2018breakfast\u2019, \u2018between breakfast and lunch\u2019) | Incident coronary heart disease (CHD); defined as non-fatal myocardial infarction (MI) or fatal CHD |\n| Kubota et al., 2016 [22] | Japan | The Japan Public Health Center-Based Prospective (JPHC) study | 82,772 | 45\u201374 | 53.3 | 12.7 | Questionnaire (self-administered) | Participants were classified into the following four groups; those who had breakfast 0 to 2 (subjects with almost never, those with 1\u20133 times/month, and those with 1\u20132 times/week were combined because of the small number of those with 1\u20133 times/month or 1\u20132 times/week), 3\u20134, 5\u20136, or 7 (everyday) times/week. Association of Skipping Breakfast with All-Cause Mortality\nTwo prospective cohort studies reported the association between skipping breakfast and the risk of all-cause death. Rong et al. [23] found a non-significant increase in the risk of all-cause death among people who never ate breakfast compared to those who consumed breakfast every day (HR 1.19, 95% CI 0.99\u20131.42). Nonetheless, a Japanese study found that skipping breakfast was associated with increased risk of all-cause death in both males (HR 1.43, 95% CI 1.21\u20131.69) and females (HR 1.34, 95% CI 1.04\u20131.73) [24]. The pooled estimates from these two studies (3 data points) showed a 32% higher risk of death from all causes among people who regularly skipped breakfast compared to those who regularly consumed it (HR 1.32, 95% CI 1.17\u20131.48; I2 = 7.6%, p = 0.339). A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis suggested a variation of the effect size from 1.24 to 1.40, which were all statistically significant towards an increased risk of all-cause death among people who regularly skipped breakfast compared to those who ate it regularly.\n4. Discussion\nNutrition plays an important role in the maintenance of overall health [25,26,27]. Recently, a statement from the American Heart Association (AHA) underscored the substantive role of meal timing and frequency in the prevention of CVDs [28].\nIn this study, we reviewed the evidence regarding the association between skipping breakfast and the prospective risk of cardiovascular morbidity and mortality and all-cause death. The magnitudes of association were variable across studies but overall suggested that people who skip breakfast may be at greater risk of experiencing adverse health outcomes compared to people who regularly consume breakfast. Specifically, we found that people who regularly skipped breakfast were about 21% more likely to suffer a CVD event or die from it, and 32% more likely to die from all causes than people who regularly ate breakfast.\nAlthough our analysis focused on studies performed within CVD primary preventive settings, two studies that reported the effect of skipping breakfast on all-cause mortality in non-primary-preventive settings also showed that skipping breakfast alone or in combination with late night dinner was associated with increased risk of all-cause death [29,30]. Among patients with ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction, skipping breakfast concomitant with late night dinner was associated with 4\u20135-fold increased likelihood of death, reinfarction, and postinfarction angina within 30 days after hospital discharge [30].\nOmitting breakfast has been associated with obesity, hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation, and may also impair serum lipids and postprandial insulin sensitivity [8,15,16,17,31,32]. Recently, skipping breakfast has also been shown to have an adverse effect on arterial stiffness and carotid atheromatic burden [33]. Furthermore, in mice, skipping breakfast has been shown to result in a disturbance of the phase peak of the clock gene, and an inability to entrain to the day/night cycle, thereby ultimately leading to disturbance of the circa
state\codex_consultations\plan_v7_codex_response.md:1486:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_092353_report.md:1:# Comparative Analysis of Adhesion Testing Methods for Polymer Coatings: Methodologies, Performance, and Regulatory Compliance ## Abstract This research report presents a comparative analysis of adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings, specifically evaluating pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. Spanning 15 sections and comprising 16,701 words, the study examines standardized test method definitions within ASTM and ISO frameworks alongside material suitability limits and thickness constraints. The methodology involves a rigorous review of ASTM D4541 protocols for pull-off testing and ASTM D903 and ISO11339 compliance for peel test variations, including 90-degree and T-peel configurations. Furthermore, the analysis addresses knife and scrape test methods for hard coatings, surface contaminants, and adhesion performance scoring scales. Key findings highlight the impact of environmental temperature and immersion effects on adhesion, alongside theoretical adhesion modeling and cohesive failure analysis. The report classifies failure modes and mechanism units while assessing urethane adhesive capabilities and bond strength applications. Administrative testing documentation and warranty requirements are also scrutinized to ensure regulatory compliance. Drawing upon 84 total unique sources cited and incorporating 218 total citations in report, this work establishes critical guidelines for selecting appropriate testing methodologies. The implications suggest that strict adherence to standardized protocols enhances reliability in predicting coating performance under varying environmental conditions, thereby informing industry best practices for quality assurance and warranty validation in polymer coating applications. ## Standardized Test Method Definitions and ASTM ISO Frameworks Adhesion testing frameworks for polymer coatings are governed by a complex array of ASTM and ISO standards that define methodological rigor and compliance thresholds [1]. Additionally, these standardized protocols establish the baseline for comparing pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials [2]. Furthermore, the regulatory landscape distinguishes between quantitative tensile measurements and qualitative visual assessments, each serving specific diagnostic purposes within the coating lifecycle [3]. Pull-off adhesion testing applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [1]. This method is utilized to gage adhesive strength of the coating once applied and cured as per manufacturer's guidelines [2]. Moreover, the pull-off adhesion tester measures adhesion of coatings to metal, wood, concrete, and rigid substrates, providing versatility across material classes [4]. Pull-off test data provides quantitative indication of bond tensile strength [5]. In addition, industrial-grade adhesives typically have tensile strength ranging from 10 MPa to over 60 MPa depending on formulation [6]. Hydroxyapatite coatings showed repeatable adhesion strength of 3.4 ± 1.0 MPa across both ASTM test methods [7]. ASTM C633-01 test uses the reported value diameter cylindrical blocks with 38 mm length for adhesion testing. Moreover, ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings [8]. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [9]. ASTM D7234 defines the standard test method for pull-off adhesion strength on concrete using portable testers [10]. Pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [11]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [11]. In addition, the Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [3]. This distinction is critical when selecting methods for epoxy, urethane, or silicone systems, as quantitative data may be required for structural warranty validation. Additionally, cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [3]. The procedure involves making lattice-pattern cuts through the coating to the substrate, applying adhesive tape, and peeling in one motion [3]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [12]. This standard is widely recognized for evaluating coating adhesion on metal substrates using the tape test method [13]. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts [13]. ASTM D3359 defines adhesion as molecular attraction and mechanical bonds between coating and substrate [14]. ASTM D3359 procedures assess the adhesion of coating films specifically on metallic substrates [15]. The tape test involves applying and removing pressure-sensitive tape over cuts made in the film [15]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [16]. Cross-Cut Test uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off [17]. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications 0B through 5B [16]. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [18]. ASTM D3359 Standard Test Method for Measuring Adhesion by Tape Test was codified in 1974 [19]. ASTM D3359 is a standard test method for measuring adhesion by tape test [20]. Crosshatch adhesion testing follows ASTM D3359 standard with grid cuts through coating [21]. ASTM D3359 is the standard method for rating adhesion by tape test [22]. Peel testing represents a third category, measuring the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate [23]. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [24]. This method is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [25]. Peel and adhesion strength testing measures bond strength when force attempts separation [25]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [26]. The peel test is a method used in materials science to measure the bond strength between two materials [27]. Peel strength is usually reported in units of force per width such as N/mm or lb/in [27]. Peel testing methods include T-peel and 90-degree peel tests standardized under ASTM D1876 and D6862 [28]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant temperature 23±1°C and 50±5% RH [29]. Peel test values represent an average between 5 and the reported value of peel for each of two tests. T-peel testing was performed according to BS 5350-C12:1994/ISO 11339:1993 standard on aluminium alloy [30]. Chinese national standard GB/T 2790-1995 specifies the 180° peel strength test method steps and requirements [31]. ISO 8510-2 covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly [24]. The T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is most widely used for thin-gauge metal adherends [26]. Table 1 summarizes the comparative frameworks for these primary adhesion testing methods, highlighting standard designations and operational limits. | Test Method | Primary Standard (ASTM/ISO) | Substrate Suitability | Measurement Type | Thickness Limit | |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- | | Pull-Off | ASTM D4541, ISO 4624 | Metal, Concrete, Rigid | Quantitative (MPa) | N/A [11] | | Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359, ISO 2409 | Metal, Plastic, Rigid | Qualitative (0B-5B) | 250 µm [32] | | Peel | ASTM D1876, ISO 11339 | Flexible-to-Rigid | Quantitative (N/mm) | N/A [28] | | Knife/Scrape | ASTM D6677 | Hard Coatings | Qualitative/Force | N/A [22] | Thickness constraints significantly influence method selection for polymer coatings. The Cross-Cut Test is limited by coating thickness with a maximum of 250 micrometers [32]. Cross-cut test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 µm [33]. This limitation renders the cross-cut method unsuitable for thick urethane or epoxy linings often exceeding this threshold. The test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [34]. Test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [34]. Adhesion pull-off strength testing determines the overall quality and integrity of an installed coating system [35]. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness [22]. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal [36]. ASTM D6677 is the standard method for evaluating adhesion by knife [22]. Cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [33]. The crosscut adhesion test is a standard ASTM or ISO procedure for dry coatings [37]. Conflicting evidence exists regarding the fundamental capabilities of these tests and specific procedural dimensions. One source states the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [4]. However, conflicting evidence indicates the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [34]. This discrepancy suggests a distinction between practical field adhesion and theoretical interfacial strength. Regarding cut patterns, evidence states cross-cut test uses a tool to cut a right-angle lattice pattern through the coating to the substrate [1]. Other evidence indicates X-cut tape test uses a sharp tool to make an X-shaped cut through the coating to substrate [4]. ASTM D3359 accommodates both via Method A (X-Cut) and Method B (Cross-Cut Tape Test), with Method B preferred for laboratory or controlled environments [12]. Test Method A (X-Cut Tape Test) is designed primarily for field use with X-shaped cut in coating. Cross-Hatch Test is typically used on witness samples of the same substrate material [38]. Procedural variations also appear in peel test specimen dimensions. Evidence indicates DOWSIL ALLGUARD coating adhesion test uses cheesecloth strip 1 inch by 12 inches embedded in wet coating [39]. In conflict, another source states peel test uses 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in coating base layer [40]. These variations likely reflect specific manufacturer protocols versus general standardized methods. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [5]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [41]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements are proposed as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [41]. Adhesion testing mechanics are complicated when polymer adhesive and metal substrate have different elastic moduli [42]. This complexity is particularly relevant for silicone and urethane coatings which exhibit different elastic properties compared to epoxy systems. Peel and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [14]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [14]. Common adhesion measuring techniques are performed using a knife or a pull-off adhesion tester. Specific material considerations emerge within the evidence regarding epoxy and silicone systems. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [43]. This suggests environmental conditioning impacts epoxy coating performance assessment. The majority of bond systems underwent tensile adhesion strength testing using a button-to-plate configuration [44]. Silicone contamination impacts bond performance, necessitating rigorous testing protocols [44]. Video How to Perform an Adhesion Test Patch provides more information on roof coating adhesion tests [45]. This indicates field verification is critical for urethane and silicone roof applications. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [46]. Adhesive performance evaluation after testing includes checking bonding strength, color stability, surface integrity, and chemical stability [47]. Tensile or shear tests measure strength between bonded materials for adhesives [48]. The study objective was to identify an optimal adhesive for the pull-off test providing higher adhesion [9]. Pull-off method provides values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [11]. The proposed adhesion testing method uses direct pull-off strength measurements at various corrosion stages [49]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site [50]. Field adhesion test also called hand pull test verifies proper sealant installation. There is no universally accepted test for conformal coating adhesion [38]. SAE J400 standard outlines specialized coating adhesion test method using Instron testing machine [13]. Latter two ASTM methods evaluate tensile or pull-off strength of coating [14]. The test method was originally developed for ductile coatings applied to metal substrates [16]. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria on page 35 to confirm proper surface preparation [51]. Nitto Denko employs adhesive pull strength evaluation using JIS Z0237 and ASTM standards [52]. Mechanical analysis services include testing for adhesive strength, tensile strength, yield stress. All structural applications must be tested by the Dow test lab for surface preparation recommendations. A similar testing program is needed for concrete coatings to enable laboratory verification. Adhesive testing products include epoxy glue, polyurethane glue, and organic silicone among many others [47]. Tensile bulk tests were performed using long dogbone specimens to characterize adhesives at room temperature [53]. Higher yield strength values obtained in compression test than in tensile test for all adhesives. Study of size dependence of bulk specimen was carried out to evaluate scale factors in adhesive testing. NPL and partners developed a tape peel test applicable to PCB or component surfaces [54]. ATS offers ASTM paint adhesion testing to determine adhesive performance according to industry standards. Peel tests involve pulling flexible material from a surface at defined angles [48]. **Key Findings** * Pull-off methods (ASTM D4541, ISO 4624) provide quantitative tensile strength data suitable for thick epoxy and urethane systems, whereas cross-cut tests (ASTM D3359) offer qualitative ratings limited to coatings under 250 µm [32]. * Conflicting evidence exists regarding whether pull-off tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or practical bond integrity, indicating a need for method selection based on specific performance criteria [4][34]. * Peel testing standards (ASTM D1876, ISO 11339) quantify force per width (N/mm) for flexible-to-rigid bonds, essential for silicone and urethane membrane evaluations [28][27]. * ASTM D3359 encompasses both X-cut and lattice patterns, with the lattice method preferred for laboratory precision and the X-cut for field applications [1]. * Environmental conditions and substrate elastic moduli significantly influence test outcomes, requiring controlled temperature and humidity during peel and tensile assessments [42][29]. ! Adhesion strength of coating to substrate defines overall coating integrity and performance [55]. Significantly, the applicability of specific adhesion testing methods varies significantly based on the physical properties of the coating material and the thickness of the applied layer. Crosshatch testing is most suitable for thin coatings on rigid substrates [21]. Thickness constraints serve as a primary determinant for method selection across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. Force measured in MPa or psi reveals the coating's tensile pull-off strength indicating adhesion [36]. Most concrete coating adhesion pull-off strength testing exhibits substrate failure in the 400-500 psi range [35]. The adhesion pull-off strength of the polyaspartic published in its data sheet is 900 psi [35]. Material suitability extends beyond thickness to include chemical composition and mechanical stiffness. Polyurethane coatings can possess higher stiffness than the adhesive used to fix the testing dolly in place [58]. Polyurethane coatings offer super flexibility, abrasion resistance, and UV stability for outdoor applications [5]. Increasing hard segment content in waterborne polyurethane formulation usually increases peel strength [31]. Epoxy coatings must conform to MPI ASM No. 116 with polyamide-cured epoxy-resin material for finish [59]. Epoxy bonded to silicone duplex coating pull-off force thickness dependence agreed with modified Kendall model for elastomeric coatings [56]. Category 4 silicone-resin coatings must contain minimum the reported value silicone resin and maximum 49 percent. Methodological distinctions between qualitative and quantitative evaluation impact data reliability for these materials. Peel strength is calculated using force applied during peeling process divided by film width. This limitation suggests that while pull-off testing measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface, it may not isolate interfacial chemistry. Conflicting evidence exists regarding specific procedural dimensions and measurement capabilities. This discrepancy indicates variability in specimen preparation standards across different manufacturers and material types. Surface prep chemistry shapes coating outcomes ranging from adhesion to corrosion resistance and durability [60]. Failure to verify and document adhesion results may result in adhesion loss not covered by Dow Warranty [51]. Tensile modulus was measured for three polyurethane coatings, an epoxy adhesive, and a cyanoacrylate adhesive [58]. Significantly, differences in Young's modulus between test methods and manufacturer data reached up to 70% for methods [61]. Adhesive bonds can be stronger than the materials being bonded together in some automotive applications. Tungsten carbide coating adhesion exceeded epoxy adhesive strength, limiting measurement to 95 MPa maximum [7]. Cohesive strength decreases as coating undergoes weathering and pull-off strength monitors breakdown [62]. The electronics industry has demanding applications with specific adhesive performance requirements. Adhesives and potting compounds are used for die attach, glob top encapsulation, underfill, edge bonding [63]. Market expectations for removable adhesives specify 1.75 pli peel strength after 3 weeks at 70C [57]. Key peel test metrics include initial peak force, average force across entire seal, and peel strength [24]. 3M 681 tape was found to most closely reflect desired properties and performance for adhesion testing [19]. Cross-cut testing measures coating resistance to separation by cutting a right-angle lattice pattern to the substrate [23]. Acceptable adhesion shows cohesive tear within sealant or elongation beyond movement capability [50]. Mode of failure and pull-off pressure confirm proper surface preparation and coating application [2]. Failure mode for BTSE/GPS coated surface is cohesive failure when specimen is dry with fracture surface [64]. Pull-off strength important parameter when selecting polymer coating systems [62]. Coating application quality varies by coater and impacts adhesion [43]. Two coats necessary to achieve 10-mil minimum dry film thickness for coating [39]. Technology assessment analyzed 94 individual coatings and 44 coating systems for application and durability [65]. First-cost comparisons rank acrylic least expensive, silicone close behind, polyurethane higher [66]. Polyurethane coatings provide high abrasion resistance, good flexibility, chemical resistance, and UV stability [59]. * Cross-cut testing is constrained to thinner coatings, with maximum limits identified at 250 micrometers or under 5 mils, whereas pull-off methods accommodate thicker duplex systems ranging up to 638 micrometers [56]. * Polyurethane and silicone materials require specific consideration of stiffness and resin content, as polyurethane coatings can exceed the stiffness of testing adhesives and silicone-resin coatings mandate minimum 22 percent resin composition [58]. * Method selection dictates data type, with cross-cut tests providing qualitative evaluation via lattice patterns and pull-off tests delivering quantitative tensile strength measurements in MPa or psi [36]. * Procedural conflicts exist in specimen preparation, evidenced by divergent peel test strip dimensions of 1x6 inches versus 1x12 inches depending on the specific coating manufacturer standards [40][39]. Specifically, *Figure 2: Bar chart displaying document engagement metrics such as views and ratings. | Published Year | 2019 | year |. *Table 1: Key Timeline Events for ASTM C1624*. ## Pull-Off Testing Mechanical Application and Procedures Pull-off testing represents a primary quantitative methodology for evaluating bond strength, distinct from qualitative assessments found in cross-cut or peel configurations. This perpendicular application differentiates pull-off mechanics from peel tests, which measure the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate at specific angles [23]. In particular, for polymer coatings such as epoxy, urethane, and silicone, the choice of mechanical application directly influences the interpretability of adhesion strength regarding regulatory compliance and field performance. The core mechanism utilizes a fixture, often referred to as a dolly, which is adhesively bonded to the cured coating surface [36]. Once bonded, the tester exerts a pulling force until failure occurs, providing a quantitative indication of bond tensile strength. This mechanical setup allows for the direct determination of adhesion strength at the metallisation or polymer interface from the substrate [67]. The mechanical distinction is critical for the research question comparing methods across materials; pull-off tests are typical for industrial coatings including polymer coatings and epoxy resins, whereas cross-cut tests are especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic. Standardization governs the mechanical procedures to ensure comparability across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. The evolution of these standards reflects the industry's shift toward quantitative evaluation methods. The following table outlines key standards and their establishment or revision dates relevant to mechanical adhesion testing procedures. | Standard Designation | Method Type | Establishment/Revision Year | Evaluation Type |. | ASTM D3359 | Tape Test | 1974 [19] | Qualitative |. | JIS K 5600-5-6 | Cross-Cut Test | 1999 | Qualitative |. | JIS K 5600-5-7 | Pull-Off Method | 2014 | Quantitative |. | ISO 4624 | Pull-Off Adhesion | 2023 [9] | Quantitative [9] |. The temporal progression in the table indicates a standardization trend toward quantitative tensile testing methods like ISO 4624 and JIS K 5600-5-7. However, conflicting evidence exists regarding the specific scope and dates of Japanese Industrial Standards. One source specifies JIS K 5600-5-7: 2014 for the Pull-Off Method as a coating tensile adhesion test for quantitative evaluation. Another source indicates JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards. While the conflict detector flags this as a contradiction, the evidence suggests these represent different parts of the same standard framework addressing different mechanical methods rather than mutually exclusive facts. The 1999 date applies to cross-cut mechanics, while the 2014 date applies to pull-off mechanics, highlighting the divergent procedural requirements within the same regulatory body. Similarly, distinctions in ASTM standards clarify the mechanical application differences. These standards support different mechanical actions; the tape test relies on adhesive removal force, while the knife test evaluates resistance to scraping. Quantitative outcomes from pull-off mechanics offer specific metrics for epoxy and silicone materials. Specific material data shows a button pull-off test measured exterior paint average bond strength at 7.27 MPa (1050 psi) [68]. These numerical values underscore the quantitative advantage of pull-off mechanics over the 0B to 5B classification range found in cross-cut adhesion test evaluation [18]. For silicone rubber and epoxy resin interfaces, pull-off tests were applied to estimate adhesion effect, demonstrating utility across these specific polymer types [69]. Mechanical application procedures also dictate sample validity. This requirement ensures that the measured force reflects coating adhesion rather than adhesive failure between the dolly and the coating. This mechanical difference implies that peel tests may better simulate edge failures in flexible silicone or urethane coatings, while pull-off tests better simulate normal stress failures in rigid epoxy systems. Environmental and procedural factors further influence mechanical test outcomes. These factors are critical when comparing methods across materials, as urethane and silicone adhesives may cure differently under varying conditions. In particular, relying solely on one mechanical method, such as the cross-cut test which uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off, may not capture the full performance profile of complex polymer systems [17]. Field application mechanics differ from laboratory settings. A simple qualitative pull test can be performed by hand by pulling silicone with reasonable strength. Nevertheless, this mismatch affects stress distribution during pull-off or peel procedures. Regulatory compliance often dictates the mechanical method selected. Note that while these standards evaluate peel strength, the mechanical action is tensile pull-off, distinguishing it from flexible peel testing defined by ISO 8510-2 which covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly. These distinctions are vital for the research question, as epoxy coatings typically require tensile pull-off (ASTM D4541), while flexible silicone or urethane membranes may require peel configurations (ASTM D1876). Cross-cut test classification based on percentage of area affected by coating removal provides a visual metric distinct from force measurements [68]. This prohibition underscores the necessity of method-specific benchmarks when comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. * Pull-off testing applies perpendicular tensile force via a dolly to provide quantitative adhesion strength values in MPa or psi, distinguishing it from qualitative cross-cut methods. * Standardization trends show a progression from qualitative tape tests (ASTM D3359, 1974) to quantitative pull-off methods (ISO 4624, 2023), influencing regulatory compliance for polymer coatings. * Mechanical suitability varies by material, with pull-off tests typical for epoxy resins and rigid substrates, while peel tests are suitable for flexible coatings experiencing prying or bending [25]. * Peel and pull-off data mechanisms differ significantly, preventing direct comparison of strength values across these testing methodologies. !
